Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I'm 29 and juggling so much outside of school. I tried to carve out about 25 hours a week, but it rarely happened in neat blocks. I'm a visual learner, so I'd print out diagrams and charts when I could afford the printing costs, then study those on my commute or during breaks at work. Most of my actual learning happened late at night when everyone else was asleep and I could finally focus.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could manage consistent evening study sessions, but when work got demanding or personal stuff came up, everything shifted to last-minute cramming before deadlines. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but honestly, I studied whenever I could grab time, which wasn't always predictable.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about an assignment and realize I'd lost track of time. Occasionally I logged in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed, but mostly it was deadline-driven. With 13 courses to manage, I couldn't afford to log in without a specific purpose.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a normal routine for me, honestly. My life is too chaotic for that kind of consistency. There were maybe a few weeks mid-semester where I tried to log in every other day, but that fell apart quickly when stress piled up. The LMS always felt like something I had to force myself to use rather than something that fit naturally into my day.
Q5: The visual resources were everything to me—slides with diagrams, video lectures I could pause and rewatch, anything with charts or infographics. I learn best when I can see concepts mapped out visually, so those resources stuck with me. The videos were helpful even though I often only watched about ten minutes at a time before downloading or screenshotting the key parts to study offline later.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums entirely. I know I have zero posts, and I feel guilty about it, but with my anxiety levels and limited time, putting myself out there felt impossible. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings because I simply couldn't afford the time or energy to go beyond what was absolutely necessary for assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could directly see how it connected to an assignment or exam. With limited resources and high stress, I had to be ruthlessly practical about where I spent my energy. If something had a clear payoff—like practice problems similar to exam questions or visual summaries of key concepts—I was all in. Abstract or open-ended activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford.
Q8: Optional activities honestly felt like traps to me. I knew they might help, but I was already drowning trying to complete the required work across 13 courses. Required activities had deadlines and consequences, which gave me the external pressure I needed to actually do them. Optional things just added to my anxiety because I knew I should do them but couldn't.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, screenshot slides, and print things out when I could. I'd study from handwritten notes on the bus, review printed diagrams during lunch breaks, and use my phone to look up concepts on free websites. The LMS was more like a starting point where I gathered materials, but the actual learning happened elsewhere.
Q10: There were countless times I'd spend hours with printed materials or downloaded content, working through problems by hand, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it from a downloaded file five more times offline. My notebook is full of visual maps and summaries I created myself, but the system only saw that initial ten-minute video view.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video or PDF once, download it immediately, and then that was my primary study material for days or weeks. My internet access is decent but I preferred having things saved locally so I could study during commutes or when I was too anxious to be online. One view in the system might represent twenty hours of actual engagement with that material.
Q12: There were several really dark periods this semester. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to even open the LMS because seeing all my overdue tasks made everything worse. Financial stress would hit and I'd have to pick up extra work shifts, which meant missing classes and falling further behind. The sadness would settle in and some days I just couldn't make myself care about any of it.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by anxiety, I'd log in with good intentions but just stare at the screen unable to process anything. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away—I'm not a traditional student with time to spare. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I did manage to access materials. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open but be too mentally depleted to actually learn anything.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open readings and scroll through them without anything sticking because my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or feeling overwhelmed. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize I'd zoned out for half of it. Those sessions where I was physically present but mentally checked out were frustrating because I knew I was wasting precious time I didn't have.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because my anxiety made it terrifying to put my thoughts out there for others to judge. I'd avoid starting big assignments because the overwhelm would freeze me, even though I knew waiting made everything worse. Sometimes I'd avoid the LMS entirely for days because seeing how behind I was triggered such intense stress that avoidance felt like self-preservation.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage consistently. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before due dates, cramming everything into the last possible hours. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and let things slide. The deadline panic was awful for my stress levels but it was also the only reliable motivator I had when everything else felt impossible.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a brief boost of hope that maybe I could actually do this. My assignment scores were pretty strong when I managed to submit, which surprised me given how chaotic my process was. But negative or critical feedback would send me spiraling, making it harder to engage with the next task. I learned to check feedback only when I was in a stable mental state.
Q18: The structure of having 13 courses simultaneously was honestly brutal. I couldn't give any single course the attention it deserved. The gamified task intervention actually helped me a bit—breaking things into smaller achievements made the mountain feel slightly more climbable. But overall, the sheer volume of content and deadlines across all courses made deep engagement with any one of them nearly impossible.
Q19: Courses with clear visual materials and well-organized modules were much easier for me to engage with. When I could see exactly what was expected and access resources quickly, I felt less overwhelmed. Courses with confusing navigation or walls of text were harder because my anxiety would spike trying to figure out what I was supposed to do. The gamified elements helped make tasks feel more manageable and less threatening.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly, clicking around, trying to engage but being too anxious or exhausted to actually produce anything. High activity might mean someone is struggling to understand what's expected, or they're paralyzed by perfectionism and can't bring themselves to submit imperfect work. Sometimes presence doesn't equal productivity, especially when mental health is involved.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance was terrible and my LMS activity was moderate at best, but I still earned a Merit because I studied intensively offline and put real effort into assignments when I could focus. The system doesn't see the hours I spent with printed materials or the quality of my offline engagement. Low visible activity doesn't necessarily mean low learning—it might mean learning happens elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone logs in and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent engagement. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention during online sessions, and the emotional or mental state behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone watched a video while crying from stress or if they truly absorbed the material. The human experience gets flattened into clicks.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and caring about understanding it—not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's when I'm curious and invested, when studying feels meaningful rather than just obligatory. For me, real engagement often happens in quiet moments with my printed notes, not necessarily in front of a screen. It's a mental and emotional state, not just a behavior.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought to stay in this program despite everything working against me. It can't capture the nights I studied through tears, or the pride I felt when concepts finally clicked. It doesn't show that I'm a 29-year-old with limited resources who's trying to build a better future despite high anxiety and constant stress. My resilience, my determination, my growth as a person—none of that shows up in login counts or video minutes.
